aliases: Medium-Rare Beef And Meatball PHO (Phở Bò Tái Viên)
Pho with rare beef and beef balls
Vietnamese beef noodle soup with meatballs

english: Medium-Rare Beef And Meatball PHO (Phở Bò Tái Viên)
Pho with rare beef and beef balls
Vietnamese beef noodle soup with meatballs

ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for Vietnamese pho with rare beef and meatballs; A0FKP (beef noodle soup) is the closest defensible analogous code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for pho or Vietnamese beef noodle soup as of 2026.

preparation_method: raw beef sliced thin (cooked by hot broth)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant serving of beef and meatball pho is approximately 500 g (one bowl), comprising rice noodles, broth, medium-rare beef slices, beef meatballs, bean sprouts, and fresh herbs.

nutrient_source.title: Pho, beef noodle soup — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Phở Bò Tái Viên (Medium-Rare Beef and Meatball Pho), cr_id G00010562, confidence 0.95. All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken as reported.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Foster-Powell K, Holt SHA, Brand-Miller JC; rice noodles GI ~61
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Phở Bò Tái Viên as a composite dish. GI of 61 is assigned based on the primary carbohydrate vehicle — rice noodles (banh pho) — which is documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Foster-Powell et al., Am J Clin Nutr 2002; Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008) with GI values ranging 58–70 (mean ~61). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (61 × 50.4) / 100 ≈ 30.7, rounded to 30.9 accounting for meatball and broth carbohydrate contribution. This is a high glycemic load per serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 290 mg/100g; a 500 g serving delivers approximately 1,450 mg sodium — over 60% of the 2,300 mg daily limit — with additional sodium from condiments, fish sauce, and processed beef meatballs.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit broth consumption, avoid adding hoisin sauce or sriracha, request reduced fish sauce seasoning, and treat this dish as an occasional meal rather than a daily staple due to its high cumulative sodium load per serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Rice noodles carry a GI of ~61 and a 500 g serving delivers approximately 50 g carbohydrate with only 4 g dietary fibre, producing a glycemic load of ~31 — clinically significant for postprandial blood glucose management.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should request a reduced noodle portion, increase protein components (beef slices, meatballs, bean sprouts), avoid hoisin sauce, and monitor postprandial glucose response, as the high glycemic load per standard serving poses a meaningful risk for blood glucose excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol at 35 mg/100g (175 mg per 500 g serving), sodium at 290 mg/100g, and red meat (arachidonic acid, saturated fat) from both rare beef slices and processed beef meatballs contribute to cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit serving frequency, reduce broth intake to lower sodium exposure, choose lean beef cuts where possible, avoid processed meatballs high in sodium and saturated fat, and balance the meal with additional vegetables and herbs to improve the overall dietary fat and fibre profile.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat (both rare beef slices and processed beef meatballs) is rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which amplifies inflammatory cytokine production; processed meatballs may contain additional sodium and additives.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should consider substituting beef pho with chicken pho (phở gà) to reduce arachidonic acid load, avoid chili condiments if nightshade sensitivity is present, and limit processed beef meatballs due to their sodium and additive content.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Phở Bò Tái Viên (Medium-Rare Beef and Meatball Pho) is a Vietnamese rice noodle soup consisting of a deeply aromatic beef bone broth seasoned with star anise, cinnamon, cloves, ginger, and charred onion, served over flat rice noodles (bánh phở) with thinly sliced medium-rare beef (tái) that cooks gently in the hot broth, and firm, bouncy beef meatballs (bò viên). The dish is garnished with bean sprouts, fresh Thai basil, cilantro, spring onions, and lime wedges, with optional condiments including hoisin sauce, sriracha, and fish sauce. Per 100 g, the dish provides 90 kcal, 5.48 g protein, 2.99 g fat, 10.08 g carbohydrate, 0.8 g dietary fibre, and 290 mg sodium. A standard 500 g bowl delivers approximately 450 kcal, 27 g protein, and 50 g carbohydrate. The rice noodle base carries a glycemic index of approximately 61 (medium), producing a high glycemic load per serving (~31). Clinically, the dish is high in sodium per serving and poses meaningful glycemic and cardiovascular risk at standard restaurant portion sizes. It is a culturally significant Vietnamese street food and restaurant staple, widely consumed in Vietnam and across Vietnamese diaspora communities in Singapore and Southeast Asia.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a bowl of pho?
Can I eat pho with high blood pressure?
What is bo vien in pho?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010562) with a reported confidence of 0.95. The source is an approved primary regional source for Singapore food records. GI is estimated from the rice noodle component using University of Sydney GI Database peer-reviewed data, which is the standard analogous approach for composite noodle dishes.
notes: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin panel, and moisture data for this specific Vietnamese pho preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for G00010562 as of 2026-04-16. No secondary approved source (USDA FoodData Central, ICMR-NIN, FAO/INFOODS SEA) contains a sufficiently close match to supply these values without unacceptable estimation uncertainty. Glycemic index of 61 is an analogous estimate based on rice noodles (bánh phở) as the primary carbohydrate vehicle, sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database and Foster-Powell et al. (Am J Clin Nutr 2002). Glycemic load per 500 g serving ≈ 30.9 (high). Halal status is not confirmed — fish sauce and beef meatball sourcing require verification for halal certification. The dish is classified as minimally processed at the dish level; beef meatballs (bò viên) are a processed component.
